S. Kloster, "Cum Gallus amore peribat. Der Tod des praefectus
Aegypti im Spiegel des 10 Eclogue," in Das antike Rom und der Osten . Festschrift
für Klaus Parlasca zum 65 . Geburtstag . Christoph Börker, Michael Donderer
(eds.). Erlangen-Nürnberg 1990, 103-123. Rev: Rassart-Debergh, Latomus 52
(1993) 758-759.
Wills's article treats Eclogue 3.1 and Numitorius' parody in Antibucolica
(Donatus, Vita 43) under three headings: The Rustic Question, The Archaic Question, and the Comic Question, (Terence, And. 763), along with the major influence
of Theocritus' fourth and fifth Idylls. Menalcas' question reappears in Eclogue
5.87 indicating its emblematic importance as "striking introduction to the
amoebean contumelies with their comedic overtones" (11). Paschalis studies Eel.
6.82-84 and provides additional evidence for the allusion to Hyacinthus and a
hitherto unnoticed allusion to Daphne's story implicit in laurus (83). The stories of
Hyacinthus and Daphne at the close of Silenus' recital accent the themes of
metamorphosis and love.

331-347; Diskussionsbericht 433-435.
Anderson's articles accent Ovidian variations on Vergilian form and manner. He is particularly concerned with gesture and prayer in the Aeneid and with
abortive gesture and prayer in the Metamorphoses. "What (Ovid) does with the
frustrated gesture of Vergil's Cassandra (Aen. 2.405-406) and with the pleading
hand of Turnus (Aen. 12.930-932, 938-939), which he perverts into the bleeding
stump of Pentheus, epitomizes the grim inefficacy of prayer in the Metamorphoses ." (177) Desmond regards Ovid's Heroides 7 as representative of readers'
response to Vergil's epic version. Privitera examines Ovid, Amores 3.9.7-14, and
Metamorphoses 15.435-438. Scarda examines Aen. 4.51 8ff and Metamorphoses
7.182-183. Tissol studies Metamorphoses 13 and 14 where Ovid replaces Vergilian
thematic hallmarks for a satirical reworking of the prophetic books, Aeneid 3 and
6, and eliminates cosmic order in favor of the universal prevalence of flux.

raw_text:
Theodore Ziolkowski, Virgil and the Moderns. Princeton, 1993.
Colakis argues that Burgess systematically subverts Aeneas' leadership
qualities and heroic destiny in his antihero, Richard Ennis, sergeant and amateur
musician in the Army Vocational and Cultural Corps ("Arma Virumque Cano"
Corps) stationed in Gibraltar in 1943. Ziolkowski' s comparative analysis highlights
American and European writing between 1914 and 1945 when Vergilian patterns,
images and values surfaced impressively in works by Auden, Broch, Eliot, Frost
and Gide, Tate, Ungaretti, Valéry and Wilder.
